title: “Profit” for a Few; “Curses” from Many!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Full-page advertisements have appeared in all the major newspapers sympathetic to the AIADMK, announcing Chief Minister Jayalalithaa's participation in the inauguration of the Chennai City Police Commissioner's office building. Today, full-page advertisements have served as a fitting reward for the page after page of newspaper coverage about the Chief Minister attending the function, inaugurating the building, and presenting awards two days earlier to twenty police personnel. It is said that advertising hoardings had been erected on both sides of the road to such an extent that no one could even walk along it!

Everyone who sees the advertisement for the inauguration of the Police Commissioner's office, particularly our friends in the police department, will inevitably recall all the details concerning that building.

On 23 March 2007, page 12 of the Budget Statement read by the Professor during the DMK administration stated: “This Government has approved the construction of an additional building for the office of the Director General of Police at an estimated cost of Rs. 20 crore, and a building for the office of the Chennai City Police Commissioner at an estimated cost of Rs. 21 crore.”

And it was not this building alone. During the DMK administration, a new annex measuring 1.61 lakh square feet was constructed in the office complex of the Director General of Police at a cost of Rs. 24.50 crore and was handed over to the police department.

Similarly, approval was granted for the construction of a new building for the Chennai City Police Commissioner's office within the Traffic Police complex at Vepery, at a cost of Rs. 25.46 crore. Construction proceeded rapidly. In 2011, when only a few months of work remained and an inauguration was being considered, the dates for the general election were announced, and consequently the inauguration did not take place. After the AIADMK won the election, they left the building unopened for all these months. Last month, on 6 September 2013, I wrote that several police station buildings, including those at Muthialpet and Sembium, had remained unopened for months while awaiting a date from the Chief Minister.
On 17 September 2013, news reports stated that the Chief Minister had inaugurated through video conference several police stations across Tamil Nadu that had until then remained unopened.

Even after the new police stations had been constructed, police personnel had been working in various dilapidated buildings, risking their lives because a date from the Chief Minister was unavailable. They silently thanked me for writing about their plight. The same was true of the Chennai City Police Commissioner's office building: it had been completed many months earlier, but they had given it no thought. After I wrote about it repeatedly, the building has now finally seen the light of day. Full-page advertisements announcing its inauguration by the Chief Minister, as well as reports about the event, have appeared in the newspapers.

But has even one newspaper written a single line explaining under which administration the building was planned, when its construction was completed, or how many months it had remained unopened? Regretfully, I must state that none has done so.

These were not the only buildings constructed during the DMK administration. A new multistorey building for the Crime Branch-Criminal Investigation Department at the Police Commissioner's office in Egmore was built at a cost of Rs. 10.17 crore. A dedicated building for the Tamil Nadu Uniformed Services Recruitment Board was constructed in the Chennai City Police Commissioner's office complex at a cost of Rs. 6.54 crore. New District Police Offices for Salem, Tiruvarur, the Nilgiris, Madurai and Cuddalore districts were built at a cost of Rs. 18.82 crore. The Tirunelveli City Police Office building was constructed for Rs. 3.85 crore. New Crime Branch-Criminal Investigation Department office buildings were built in Vellore, Tirunelveli, Thoothukudi, Virudhunagar and Erode districts at a cost of Rs. 1.75 crore. Office buildings for the Special Branch and the Criminal Investigation Department were built under the Police Modernisation Scheme. The administrative office, barracks and warehouse buildings for the Second Battalion of the Tamil Nadu Special Police at Avadi were all constructed during the DMK administration.

There were 219 new police station buildings constructed at a cost of Rs. 67.24 crore; new buildings for 92 police stations that had been functioning in rented premises; and buildings for the Directorate of Vigilance and Anti-Corruption. All of them were built during the DMK administration!

During the preceding five years, the DMK administration sanctioned the construction of 11,029 police quarters at a total cost of Rs. 683.19 crore. Of these, 8,254 quarters were completed at a cost of Rs. 411.85 crore, while construction of the remaining quarters was under way.

In 2010-11, under the DMK administration, 2,000 quarters were constructed at a cost of Rs. 136.94 crore in various parts of the State, including Arani in Tiruvannamalai district; Keeranur and Chathirapatti in Dindigul district; Natrampalli in Vellore district; and Singampunari in Sivaganga district. This too was accomplished under the DMK administration!
A training centre for the Women's Commando Force was completed in the Marudham complex in Chennai at a cost of Rs. 1.44 crore and handed over to the police department.
The first phase of construction of the Tamil Nadu Police Academy at Oonamanchery was completed and handed over to the police department during the DMK administration.

All these were efforts undertaken by the DMK administration to provide the police department with the buildings it required. But were buildings the only things provided to police personnel during the DMK administration? Shall I briefly outline a few of the other achievements made for them under the DMK government?

Whenever a Police Commission was constituted for the welfare of police personnel, it happened only during periods of DMK rule. In 1969, when I assumed office as Chief Minister for the first time, I established, under the chairmanship of R. A. Gopalaswami Iyengar, ICS, the first Police Commission ever constituted by a State Government in India. Of the Commission's 133 recommendations, 115 were immediately accepted and implemented. In 1989, when the DMK formed the government for the third time, a second Police Commission was established under the chairmanship of P. Sabanayagam, IAS. It submitted 112 recommendations.

Although the DMK government was dismissed within two years, 87 of those recommendations had already been implemented. Again, it was during the DMK administration of 2006 that a third Police Commission was constituted under the chairmanship of Pooranalingam, IAS. Of its 444 recommendations, 278 were accepted in principle by the DMK government. Government orders were issued on 48 of them, while a further 166 recommendations were being implemented by departmental heads.
Has any administration other than a DMK government ever constituted a Police Commission for the welfare of police personnel? No.

From 1 October 2008, the DMK administration introduced a system under which police personnel could purchase commodities at half price through fair-price shops near their residential areas. Nearly 60,000 police families continue to benefit from that scheme even now.

It was also a DMK administration that issued the historic order in 1973 admitting women into the police department for the first time. Because women had thus been inducted into the police force, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa subsequently opened all-women police stations for the first time during an AIADMK administration. I do not wish to conceal that fact. When an opposing party has done something, I have never wished to suppress or hide it.
Page after page is now being written about financial rewards and promotions for police personnel. I wish to recall just one earlier incident.

On 12 September 2003, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa issued a statement. It said that Mayilsamy, a police constable serving in the Erode District Armed Reserve, had suddenly fallen ill and died while on security duty, and that his family would be given Rs. 1 lakh. Although it was described simply as security duty, the truth was that he had been serving on the Chief Minister's security detail. Do you know when that financial assistance of Rs. 1 lakh was provided? Fifteen months after the Chief Minister's announcement, on 22 December 2004, the file was sent to the Chief Minister's office for approval. Was it signed? The file sent on 22 December 2004 did not return from the Chief Minister's office. On 14 September 2005, a note was sent to that office requesting the return of the file and stating that the deceased Mayilsamy's mother was pressing for the payment. Was the file signed even then? No. On 24 January 2006, the Home Department prepared another file seeking approval to provide Rs. 1 lakh to the affected family and sent it to the Chief Minister's office. Although the Finance Secretary signed that file on 27 January 2006 and forwarded it to the Chief Minister's office, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa never signed and returned it during the remainder of her term. After the general election in May 2006, the file was sent to me as Chief Minister, and I signed my approval on 11 June 2006.

I placed these very details before the Legislative Assembly on 30 August 2006 during the debate on the police department's demand for grants, and they form part of the official proceedings. Even today, another report has appeared. In 2002, when police personnel shot and apprehended Imam Ali, the mentor of Police Fakhruddin, Jayalalithaa granted special awards and promotions to only some of the officers involved. It is now said that twelve police personnel were denied promotion.

Believing that the people will have forgotten all these reports, they now seek to deceive them by announcing rewards of Rs. 5 lakh and promotions for a select few police personnel. Yet even these benefits have not been properly extended to everyone who worked on that operation. By granting them only to a few, they have earned the “curses” of the others! A few receive the “profit”; many bestow the “curse”!
With affection,
M. K. 13 October 2013
